items.correct: The difference between what consumers are willing to pay and what they actually pay — represented by the area below the demand curve and above the price line.
items.misconception: Consumer surplus is leftover stock that consumers haven't bought yet.
items.random_wrong: Consumer surplus is the profit that consumers make.
items.why: 'Surplus' is misleadingly physical-sounding. It is a welfare measure — the value consumers get beyond what they paid. Students take the everyday meaning of 'surplus' and miss the economic concept.

items.q: What components make up aggregate demand?
items.correct: AD = C + I + G + (X - M): consumption, investment, government spending, and net exports. A shift in any component shifts the AD curve.
items.misconception: Aggregate demand is just the total amount consumers want to buy.
items.random_wrong: Aggregate demand is the same as GDP measured by demand.
items.why: AD covers all spending sources, not just households. Students reduce it to consumer demand, missing that investment, government and trade are equally constitutive. Mis-attributing a shift then misdiagnoses the cause.

items.correct: A sustained rise in the general price level over time — reducing the purchasing power of money. Measured by the percentage change in a price index.
items.misconception: Inflation is when prices are high.
items.random_wrong: Inflation is when the economy grows too fast.
items.why: Inflation is about RISING prices, not high prices. A country can have stable high prices (no inflation) or rising low prices (high inflation). Students confuse level with rate of change.

items.q: Alfred Marshall published 'Principles of Economics' establishing supply-demand analysis in:
items.why: It's 1890. “1776” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A fall in the price of a substitute good causes demand to:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Decrease (curve shifts left). “Increase (shifts right)” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The real figure is 1890 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
